107: function computeCompositeScore(signals, loopType) {
108:   if (!signals || typeof signals !== 'object') return 0;
109:   const clamp = (value) => Math.max(0, Math.min(1, value));
110:   const safe = (value) => (typeof value === 'number' && Number.isFinite(value) ? value : 0);
111:   if (loopType === 'research') {
112:     const normalizedDiversity = Math.min(safe(signals.sourceDiversity) / 3.0, 1.0);
113:     const normalizedDepth = Math.min(safe(signals.evidenceDepth) / 5.0, 1.0);
114:     const invertedContradictions = 1.0 - clamp(safe(signals.contradictionDensity));
115:     const score =
116:       clamp(safe(signals.questionCoverage)) * 0.30 +
117:       clamp(safe(signals.claimVerificationRate)) * 0.25 +
118:       invertedContradictions * 0.15 +
119:       normalizedDiversity * 0.15 +
120:       normalizedDepth * 0.15;
121:     return Math.round(clamp(score) * 1000) / 1000;
122:   }
123:   if (loopType === 'context') {
124:     // REUSE catalog weighted highest (reuse-first principle); agreement second
125:     // (by-model confidence); then scope coverage, relevance gate, dependency map.
126:     const score =
127:       clamp(safe(signals.reuseCatalogCoverage)) * 0.30 +
128:       clamp(safe(signals.agreementRate)) * 0.25 +
129:       clamp(safe(signals.sliceCoverage)) * 0.20 +
130:       clamp(safe(signals.relevanceFloor)) * 0.15 +
131:       clamp(safe(signals.dependencyCompleteness)) * 0.10;
132:     return Math.round(clamp(score) * 1000) / 1000;
133:   }
134:   const score =
135:     clamp(safe(signals.dimensionCoverage)) * 0.25 +
136:     clamp(safe(signals.findingStability)) * 0.20 +
137:     clamp(safe(signals.p0ResolutionRate)) * 0.25 +
138:     clamp(safe(signals.evidenceDensity)) * 0.15 +
139:     clamp(safe(signals.hotspotSaturation)) * 0.15;
140:   return Math.round(clamp(score) * 1000) / 1000;
141: }
142: 
143: function parseMetadata(metadata) {
144:   return metadata && typeof metadata === 'object' ? metadata : {};
145: }
146: 
147: function buildReviewSignals(nodes, edges) {
148:   const dimensionIds = new Set(nodes.filter((node) => node.kind === 'DIMENSION').map((node) => node.id));
149:   const findingNodes = nodes.filter((node) => node.kind === 'FINDING');
150:   const fileNodes = nodes.filter((node) => node.kind === 'FILE');
151:   const coversEdges = edges.filter((edge) => edge.relation === 'COVERS');
152:   const contradictionEdges = edges.filter((edge) => edge.relation === 'CONTRADICTS');
153:   const evidenceEdges = edges.filter((edge) => edge.relation === 'EVIDENCE_FOR');
154:   const resolvesTargetIds = new Set(edges.filter((edge) => edge.relation === 'RESOLVES').map((edge) => edge.targetId));
155:   const coveredDimensionIds = new Set(
156:     coversEdges.map((edge) => edge.sourceId).filter((sourceId) => dimensionIds.has(sourceId)),
157:   );
158:   const contradictionNodeIds = new Set();
159:   for (const edge of contradictionEdges) {
160:     contradictionNodeIds.add(edge.sourceId);
161:     contradictionNodeIds.add(edge.targetId);
162:   }
163:   const p0Findings = findingNodes.filter((node) => parseMetadata(node.metadata).severity === 'P0');
164:   const hotspotFiles = fileNodes.filter((node) => {
165:     const hotspotScore = parseMetadata(node.metadata).hotspot_score;
166:     return typeof hotspotScore === 'number' && hotspotScore > 0;
167:   });
168:   let saturatedHotspots = 0;
169:   for (const file of hotspotFiles) {
170:     const coveringDimensions = new Set(
171:       coversEdges
172:         .filter((edge) => edge.targetId === file.id)
173:         .map((edge) => edge.sourceId)
174:         .filter((sourceId) => dimensionIds.has(sourceId)),
175:     );
176:     if (coveringDimensions.size >= 2) saturatedHotspots += 1;
177:   }
178:   return {
179:     dimensionCoverage: dimensionIds.size > 0 ? coveredDimensionIds.size / dimensionIds.size : 0,
180:     findingStability: findingNodes.length > 0
181:       ? findingNodes.filter((node) => !contradictionNodeIds.has(node.id)).length / findingNodes.length
182:       : 0,
183:     p0ResolutionRate: p0Findings.length > 0
184:       ? p0Findings.filter((node) => resolvesTargetIds.has(node.id)).length / p0Findings.length
185:       : 1,
186:     evidenceDensity: findingNodes.length > 0 ? evidenceEdges.length / findingNodes.length : 0,
187:     hotspotSaturation: hotspotFiles.length > 0 ? saturatedHotspots / hotspotFiles.length : 1,
188:   };
189: }
190: 
191: function evaluateResearch(signals, gaps, contradictions, unverified) {
192:   const thresholds = {
193:     questionCoverage: 0.7,
194:     claimVerificationRate: 0.6,
195:     contradictionDensity: 0.15,
196:     sourceDiversity: 1.5,
197:     evidenceDepth: 1.5,
198:   };
199:   const blockers = [];
200:   const trace = [
201:     { signal: 'questionCoverage', value: signals.questionCoverage, threshold: thresholds.questionCoverage, passed: signals.questionCoverage >= thresholds.questionCoverage, role: 'weighted' },
202:     { signal: 'claimVerificationRate', value: signals.claimVerificationRate, threshold: thresholds.claimVerificationRate, passed: signals.claimVerificationRate >= thresholds.claimVerificationRate, role: 'weighted' },
203:     { signal: 'contradictionDensity', value: signals.contradictionDensity, threshold: thresholds.contradictionDensity, passed: signals.contradictionDensity <= thresholds.contradictionDensity, role: 'weighted' },
204:     { signal: 'sourceDiversity', value: signals.sourceDiversity, threshold: thresholds.sourceDiversity, passed: signals.sourceDiversity >= thresholds.sourceDiversity, role: 'blocking_guard' },
205:     { signal: 'evidenceDepth', value: signals.evidenceDepth, threshold: thresholds.evidenceDepth, passed: signals.evidenceDepth >= thresholds.evidenceDepth, role: 'blocking_guard' },
206:   ];
207:   if (signals.sourceDiversity < thresholds.sourceDiversity) {
208:     blockers.push({ type: 'source_diversity_guard', description: `Source diversity (${signals.sourceDiversity.toFixed(2)}) is below the blocking threshold (${thresholds.sourceDiversity}). STOP is blocked until diverse sources cover key questions.`, count: 1, severity: 'blocking' });
209:   }
210:   if (signals.evidenceDepth < thresholds.evidenceDepth) {
211:     blockers.push({ type: 'evidence_depth_guard', description: `Evidence depth (${signals.evidenceDepth.toFixed(2)}) is below the blocking threshold (${thresholds.evidenceDepth}). STOP is blocked until question->finding->source chains are deeper.`, count: 1, severity: 'blocking' });
212:   }
213:   if (gaps.length > 0) {
214:     blockers.push({ type: 'uncovered_questions', description: `${gaps.length} question(s) have no coverage edges`, count: gaps.length, severity: signals.questionCoverage < thresholds.questionCoverage ? 'blocking' : 'warning' });
215:   }
216:   if (contradictions.length > 0 && signals.contradictionDensity > thresholds.contradictionDensity) {
217:     blockers.push({ type: 'high_contradiction_density', description: `${contradictions.length} contradiction(s) detected with density above threshold`, count: contradictions.length, severity: 'blocking' });
218:   }
219:   if (unverified.length > 0) {
220:     blockers.push({ type: 'unverified_claims', description: `${unverified.length} claim(s) remain unverified`, count: unverified.length, severity: signals.claimVerificationRate < thresholds.claimVerificationRate ? 'blocking' : 'warning' });
221:   }
222:   return { blockers, trace };
223: }
224: 
225: function evaluateReview(signals, gaps, contradictions) {
226:   const thresholds = {
227:     dimensionCoverage: 0.8,
228:     findingStability: 0.7,
229:     p0ResolutionRate: 0.9,
230:     evidenceDensity: 1.0,
231:     hotspotSaturation: 0.6,
232:   };
233:   const blockers = [];
234:   const trace = [
235:     { signal: 'dimensionCoverage', value: signals.dimensionCoverage, threshold: thresholds.dimensionCoverage, passed: signals.dimensionCoverage >= thresholds.dimensionCoverage, role: 'blocking_guard' },
236:     { signal: 'findingStability', value: signals.findingStability, threshold: thresholds.findingStability, passed: signals.findingStability >= thresholds.findingStability, role: 'weighted' },
237:     { signal: 'p0ResolutionRate', value: signals.p0ResolutionRate, threshold: thresholds.p0ResolutionRate, passed: signals.p0ResolutionRate >= thresholds.p0ResolutionRate, role: 'weighted' },
238:     { signal: 'evidenceDensity', value: signals.evidenceDensity, threshold: thresholds.evidenceDensity, passed: signals.evidenceDensity >= thresholds.evidenceDensity, role: 'weighted' },
239:     { signal: 'hotspotSaturation', value: signals.hotspotSaturation, threshold: thresholds.hotspotSaturation, passed: signals.hotspotSaturation >= thresholds.hotspotSaturation, role: 'weighted' },
240:   ];
241:   if (signals.p0ResolutionRate < thresholds.p0ResolutionRate) {
242:     blockers.push({ type: 'unresolved_p0_findings', description: `P0 resolution rate (${(signals.p0ResolutionRate * 100).toFixed(0)}%) is below threshold (${(thresholds.p0ResolutionRate * 100).toFixed(0)}%)`, count: 1, severity: 'blocking' });
243:   }
244:   if (signals.dimensionCoverage < thresholds.dimensionCoverage) {
245:     blockers.push({ type: 'uncovered_dimensions', description: `Dimension coverage (${(signals.dimensionCoverage * 100).toFixed(0)}%) is below threshold (${(thresholds.dimensionCoverage * 100).toFixed(0)}%). ${gaps.length} gap(s) found. STOP is blocked until all required dimensions have meaningful coverage.`, count: gaps.length, severity: 'blocking' });
246:   }
247:   if (contradictions.length > 0 && signals.findingStability < thresholds.findingStability) {
248:     blockers.push({ type: 'unstable_findings', description: `${contradictions.length} contradiction(s) are lowering finding stability below threshold`, count: contradictions.length, severity: 'blocking' });
249:   }
250:   return { blockers, trace };
251: }
252: 
253: function evaluateContext(signals, gaps, contradictions) {
254:   const thresholds = {
255:     sliceCoverage: 0.7,
256:     reuseCatalogCoverage: 0.6,
257:     agreementRate: 0.5,
258:     relevanceFloor: 0.5,
259:     dependencyCompleteness: 0.7,
260:   };
261:   const blockers = [];
262:   const trace = [
263:     { signal: 'sliceCoverage', value: signals.sliceCoverage, threshold: thresholds.sliceCoverage, passed: signals.sliceCoverage >= thresholds.sliceCoverage, role: 'blocking_guard' },
264:     { signal: 'reuseCatalogCoverage', value: signals.reuseCatalogCoverage, threshold: thresholds.reuseCatalogCoverage, passed: signals.reuseCatalogCoverage >= thresholds.reuseCatalogCoverage, role: 'weighted' },
265:     { signal: 'agreementRate', value: signals.agreementRate, threshold: thresholds.agreementRate, passed: signals.agreementRate >= thresholds.agreementRate, role: 'blocking_guard' },
266:     { signal: 'relevanceFloor', value: signals.relevanceFloor, threshold: thresholds.relevanceFloor, passed: signals.relevanceFloor >= thresholds.relevanceFloor, role: 'blocking_guard' },
267:     { signal: 'dependencyCompleteness', value: signals.dependencyCompleteness, threshold: thresholds.dependencyCompleteness, passed: signals.dependencyCompleteness >= thresholds.dependencyCompleteness, role: 'weighted' },
268:   ];
269:   if (signals.sliceCoverage < thresholds.sliceCoverage) {
270:     blockers.push({ type: 'uncovered_slices', description: `Slice coverage (${(signals.sliceCoverage * 100).toFixed(0)}%) is below threshold (${(thresholds.sliceCoverage * 100).toFixed(0)}%). ${gaps.length} gap(s) found. STOP is blocked until the in-scope surface is swept.`, count: gaps.length, severity: 'blocking' });
271:   }
272:   if (signals.relevanceFloor < thresholds.relevanceFloor) {
273:     blockers.push({ type: 'low_relevance_focus', description: `Relevance floor (${(signals.relevanceFloor * 100).toFixed(0)}%) is below threshold. The loop is collecting mostly low-relevance context; STOP is blocked until findings clear the relevance gate.`, count: 1, severity: 'blocking' });
274:   }
275:   if (signals.agreementRate < thresholds.agreementRate) {
276:     blockers.push({ type: 'low_cross_executor_agreement', description: `Cross-executor agreement (${(signals.agreementRate * 100).toFixed(0)}%) is below threshold. Findings are not yet confirmed by multiple model lenses; STOP is blocked.`, count: 1, severity: 'blocking' });
277:   }
278:   if (contradictions.length > 0) {
279:     blockers.push({ type: 'context_contradictions', description: `${contradictions.length} contradiction(s) detected across executors; reconcile before STOP.`, count: contradictions.length, severity: 'warning' });
280:   }

<type>file</type>
<content>
1: ---
2: title: "OpenCode CLI - Agent Delegation"
3: description: "Agent routing matrix for opencode run dispatches. Documents the agent slugs available via .opencode/agents/, the cli pattern As @<agent> for prompt-time routing, and the orchestration principle: calling AI decides WHAT, agent shapes HOW."
4: trigger_phrases:
5:   - "opencode agent delegation"
6:   - "opencode agent routing matrix"
7:   - "opencode agent slug selection"
8:   - "opencode leaf agent constraints"
9:   - "opencode multi-agent workflow"
10: importance_tier: normal
11: contextType: implementation
12: ---
13: 
14: # OpenCode CLI - Agent Delegation
15: 
16: How cli-opencode routes dispatches to specialized OpenCode agents via the `--agent <slug>` flag. The calling AI decides WHAT to delegate. The agent's frontmatter shapes HOW the dispatched session processes it.
17: 
18: ---
19: 
20: ## 1. OVERVIEW
21: 
22: OpenCode agents are markdown files under `.opencode/agents/<slug>.md`. Each file's frontmatter pins the model, tool permissions, system prompt, and behavioral constraints. When `opencode run --agent <slug>` is invoked, the dispatched session inherits all of that.
23: 
24: The cli-opencode skill exposes the project's full agent roster. The calling AI selects the right slug based on the task type. The agent then shapes the dispatched session — different model, different tool permissions, different system prompt.
25: 
26: ### Discovery commands
27: 
28: | Command | Purpose |
29: |---------|---------|
30: | `opencode agent list` | List all registered agent slugs across project + user scopes |
31: | `opencode debug agent <slug>` | Print resolved frontmatter and system prompt for a slug |
32: | `opencode debug skill <slug>` | Print skill resolution if the agent loads one |
33: 
34: Project-local agents resolve from `.opencode/agents/<slug>.md`. User-level agents fall back from `~/.opencode/agents/<slug>.md` when the project file is missing. The `debug agent` subcommand prints the merged result so the calling AI can confirm which file the dispatch will load.
35: 
36: ### Orchestration principle
37: 
38: The calling AI decides WHAT to delegate. The agent's frontmatter shapes HOW the dispatched session processes the task. The calling AI always validates and integrates the output.
39: 
40: This separation of concerns is load-bearing for the cli-opencode skill: the calling AI owns the task graph and the integration step, while the dispatched OpenCode session owns the per-task execution under the agent's tool permissions and system prompt.
41: 
42: ---
43: 
44: ## 2. ORCHESTRATION MODEL
45: 
46: ```text
47: Calling AI (CONDUCTOR)
48:   |
49:   |-- Analyzes task, selects OpenCode agent slug
50:   |-- Constructs opencode run command with --agent <slug> flag
51:   |-- Delegates via Bash tool
52:   |
53:   v
54: opencode run --agent <slug> (EXECUTOR ENTRY)
55:   |
56:   |-- Resolves .opencode/agents/<slug>.md frontmatter
57:   |-- Pins model, tool permissions, system prompt, behavioral rules
58:   |-- Loads project plugins, skills, and MCP servers
59:   |
60:   v
61: OpenCode Session (AGENT EXECUTION)
62:   |
63:   |-- Agent shapes the dispatch under its frontmatter constraints
64:   |-- Streams JSON events (session.started, message.delta, tool.call,
65:   |    tool.result, session.completed) to the calling AI
66:   |-- Honors LEAF agent restrictions (no nested dispatches, read-only)
67:   |
68:   v
69: Calling AI (CONDUCTOR — RESULT HANDBACK)
70:   |
71:   |-- Parses the JSON event stream
72:   |-- Validates agent output quality
73:   |-- Integrates findings into the parent workflow
74:   |-- Decides next step (next dispatch, completion, escalation)
75: ```
76: 
77: ### Invocation pattern
78: 
79: cli-opencode dispatches use the `--agent <slug>` flag. The flag loads the agent definition from `.opencode/agents/<slug>.md` (project-local) or the user-level fallback. The dispatch shape pins model, variant, format, and working directory alongside the agent slug:
80: 
81: ```bash
82: opencode run \
83:   --model opencode-go/deepseek-v4-pro \
84:   --agent <slug> \
85:   --variant high \
86:   --format json \
87:   --dir /repo \
88:   "<prompt>"
89: ```
90: 
91: The `--variant high` flag is the cli-opencode default for cross-AI dispatches. The agent's own frontmatter may override the model selection if the agent pins a specific model. When an agent does NOT pin a model, the `--model` flag wins.
92: 
93: ### Conductor rules
94: 
95: 1. The calling AI always **decomposes** complex tasks before delegating to a single agent.
96: 2. The calling AI always **validates** OpenCode output before integrating.
97: 3. The calling AI never **blindly forwards** user requests to OpenCode without picking the right agent.
98: 4. OpenCode agents operate within their declared tool-permission boundaries — LEAF agents may not write files or dispatch sub-agents.
99: 5. If an agent returns low-quality output, the calling AI retries with refined instructions or selects a different agent.
100: 6. Each `opencode run` invocation is **stateless** by default — include all necessary context in the prompt or use `--continue` / `--session <id>` for explicit continuation.
101: 7. The calling AI surfaces the JSON event stream to the operator when relevant, especially `tool.call` events.
102: 
103: ### Orchestration boundaries
104: 
105: | Concern | Owner |
106: |---------|-------|
107: | Task selection and decomposition | Calling AI |
108: | Agent slug selection | Calling AI |
109: | Prompt construction (RCAF / TIDD-EC / etc.) | Calling AI |
110: | Model and variant selection | Calling AI (agent frontmatter may pin) |
111: | Tool permissions inside the dispatch | Agent frontmatter |
112: | System prompt and behavioral rules | Agent frontmatter |
113: | Result validation and integration | Calling AI |
114: | Memory handback extraction | Calling AI |
115: 
116: ---
117: 
118: ## 3. AGENT ROSTER
119: 
120: The repo ships these agents under `.opencode/agents/`. The cli-opencode skill can route to any of them. The summary table below is followed by per-agent property tables for the most-dispatched roles.
121: 
122: | Slug | Role | When to dispatch |
123: |------|------|-------------------|
124: | `general` | Default subagent — implementation, complex tasks | Routine multi-step work that does not match a specialist |
125: | `context` | Read-only retrieval and codebase exploration | Search, pattern discovery, context loading. LEAF agent — no sub-dispatches, no writes |
126: | `orchestrate` | Multi-agent coordination | Complex workflows that span several agents |
127: | `write` | Documentation generation | Creating READMEs, skills, guides |
128: | `review` | Code review and PR analysis | Pre-merge reviews, quality gates. READ-ONLY |
129: | `debug` | Fresh-perspective debugging | Root cause analysis after 3+ failed debug attempts. Exclusive write access for `debug-delegation.md` |
130: | `deep-research` | Iterative research loop executor | Single research-cycle dispatches. State externalized to JSONL + strategy.md. Dispatched only by `/deep:research` command |
131: | `deep-review` | Iterative code review loop executor | Single review-cycle dispatches. P0/P1/P2 findings, severity-weighted convergence. Dispatched only by `/deep:review` command |
132: | `ai-council` | Multi-strategy planning architect | Complex planning that benefits from comparing multiple solution strategies. PLANNING-ONLY |
133: | `deep-agent-improvement` | Proposal-only mutator for bounded agent improvement | Agent evaluation via `/deep:agent-improvement` command loop |
134: 
135: ---
136: 
137: ### @context — Read-Only Codebase Explorer
138: 
139: | Property | Value |
140: |----------|-------|
141: | **Role** | Read-only codebase exploration and pattern discovery |
142: | **Sandbox** | Read-only (no writes, no sub-dispatches) |
143: | **Modifies files** | Never |
144: | **LEAF constraint** | YES — single iteration, no nested dispatches |
145: 
146: **Best for:** File discovery, pattern analysis, dependency tracing, architecture mapping, gathering context before implementation.
147: 
148: **Delegate when:** The calling AI needs structured codebase exploration with read-only safety guarantees.
149: 
150: ---
151: 
152: ### @debug — Fresh-Perspective Debugger
153: 
154: | Property | Value |
155: |----------|-------|
156: | **Role** | Systematic debugging with fresh context |
157: | **Sandbox** | Workspace-write (bug fixes only) |

159: | **LEAF constraint** | No |
160: 
161: **Best for:** Bugs that resist initial debugging (3+ failed attempts), root cause analysis, reproducing elusive errors.
162: 
163: **Delegate when:** The calling AI's own debug attempts have failed. The fresh perspective avoids inheriting wrong assumptions from the parent conversation.
164: 
165: **Methodology:** Observe -> Analyze -> Hypothesize -> Fix.
166: 
167: ---
168: 
169: ### @review — Code Quality Guardian
170: 
171: | Property | Value |
172: |----------|-------|

174: | **Sandbox** | Read-only |
175: | **Modifies files** | Never |
176: | **LEAF constraint** | No |
177: 
178: **Best for:** Pre-merge reviews, security audits, P0 / P1 / P2 severity classification.
179: 
180: **Delegate when:** The calling AI wants a second perspective on code quality, or needs to validate its own implementation from a different model's viewpoint.
181: 
182: ---
183: 
184: 
185: ### @ai-council — Multi-Strategy Planning Architect
186: 
187: | Property | Value |
188: |----------|-------|
189: | **Role** | Multi-strategy planning across diverse thinking lenses |
190: | **Sandbox** | Read-only |
191: | **Modifies files** | Never |
192: | **LEAF constraint** | PLANNING-ONLY (no file modifications) |
193: 
194: **Best for:** Architecture decisions, complex planning that benefits from comparing 3+ solution strategies.
195: 
196: **Delegate when:** The calling AI needs a fundamentally different planning approach or wants to compare strategies before committing to one.
197: 
198: ---
199: 
200: ### @deep-research and @deep-review — Loop Executors
201: 
202: These two agents are dispatched only by their parent commands. The calling AI does NOT route to them directly.
203: 
204: | Slug | Parent command | Loop role |
205: |------|----------------|-----------|
206: | `deep-research` | `/deep:research` | Single research iteration with externalized JSONL + strategy.md state |
207: | `deep-review` | `/deep:review` | Single review iteration with P0/P1/P2 findings and severity-weighted convergence |
208: 
209: Both are LEAF agents. Each iteration is fresh-context. The parent command owns convergence detection and state continuity.
210: 
211: ---
212: 
213: ## 4. AGENT ROUTING MATRIX
214: 
215: Pick the agent that matches the task type. Default to `general` when no specialist fits.
216: 
217: | Task type | Agent | Invocation pattern |
218: |-----------|-------|---------------------|
219: | Codebase exploration | `context` | `opencode run --agent context --variant high --format json --dir /repo "Map the dependency graph for src/"` |
220: | Multi-agent coordination | `orchestrate` | `opencode run --agent orchestrate --variant high --format json --dir /repo "Coordinate review and testing of auth module"` |
221: | Documentation generation | `write` | `opencode run --agent write --variant high --format json --dir /repo "Generate README for the cli-opencode skill"` |
222: | Code review | `review` | `opencode run --agent review --variant high --format json --dir /repo "Review @src/auth.ts for security issues"` |
223: | Root cause debugging | `debug` | `opencode run --agent debug --variant high --format json --dir /repo "Debug this error: <error>"` |
224: | Iterative research loop | `deep-research` | **Command-only.** Dispatch via `/deep:research` (or `/deep:research:auto`). Direct `opencode run --agent deep-research` is forbidden; the parent command owns iteration state, JSONL, and convergence. <!-- F-007-B2-02 --> |
225: | Iterative code review loop | `deep-review` | **Command-only.** Dispatch via `/deep:review` (or `/deep:review:auto`). Direct `opencode run --agent deep-review` is forbidden; the parent command owns iteration state and severity-weighted convergence. <!-- F-007-B2-02 --> |
226: | Multi-strategy planning | `ai-council` | `opencode run --agent ai-council --variant high --format json --dir /repo "Plan the authentication redesign — compare three strategies."` |
227: | Agent improvement | `deep-agent-improvement` | **Command-only.** Dispatch via `/deep:agent-improvement`. Direct `opencode run --agent deep-agent-improvement` is forbidden; the parent command owns evaluation, candidates, and promotion. <!-- F-007-B2-02 --> |
228: | Default / unspecified | `general` | `opencode run --agent general --variant high --format json --dir /repo "<prompt>"` |
229: 
230: ---
231: 
232: ## 5. THE `As @<agent>` PROMPT-TIME PATTERN
233: 
234: Mirroring the sibling cli-* skills, cli-opencode supports an inline routing pattern where the calling AI prefixes the prompt with `As @<agent>:` instead of using the `--agent` flag. This is useful when the calling AI dispatches a single `opencode run` and shifts agent behavior mid-session.

245: The dispatched session parses the `As @review:` prefix and loads the review agent's frontmatter from `.opencode/agents/review.md`. The remaining prompt is passed to that agent.
246:

# OpenCode CLI - Agent Delegation

How cli-opencode routes dispatches to specialized OpenCode agents via the `--agent <slug>` flag. The calling AI decides WHAT to delegate. The agent's frontmatter shapes HOW the dispatched session processes it.

---

## 1. OVERVIEW

OpenCode agents are markdown files under `.opencode/agents/<slug>.md`. Each file's frontmatter pins the model, tool permissions, system prompt, and behavioral constraints. When `opencode run --agent <slug>` is invoked, the dispatched session inherits all of that.

The cli-opencode skill exposes the project's full agent roster. The calling AI selects the right slug based on the task type. The agent then shapes the dispatched session — different model, different tool permissions, different system prompt.

### Discovery commands

| Command | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `opencode agent list` | List all registered agent slugs across project + user scopes |
| `opencode debug agent <slug>` | Print resolved frontmatter and system prompt for a slug |
| `opencode debug skill <slug>` | Print skill resolution if the agent loads one |

Project-local agents resolve from `.opencode/agents/<slug>.md`. User-level agents fall back from `~/.opencode/agents/<slug>.md` when the project file is missing. The `debug agent` subcommand prints the merged result so the calling AI can confirm which file the dispatch will load.

### Orchestration principle

The calling AI decides WHAT to delegate. The agent's frontmatter shapes HOW the dispatched session processes the task. The calling AI always validates and integrates the output.

This separation of concerns is load-bearing for the cli-opencode skill: the calling AI owns the task graph and the integration step, while the dispatched OpenCode session owns the per-task execution under the agent's tool permissions and system prompt.

---

## 2. ORCHESTRATION MODEL

```text
Calling AI (CONDUCTOR)
  |
  |-- Analyzes task, selects OpenCode agent slug
  |-- Constructs opencode run command with --agent <slug> flag
  |-- Delegates via Bash tool
  |
  v
opencode run --agent <slug> (EXECUTOR ENTRY)
  |
  |-- Resolves .opencode/agents/<slug>.md frontmatter
  |-- Pins model, tool permissions, system prompt, behavioral rules
  |-- Loads project plugins, skills, and MCP servers
  |
  v
OpenCode Session (AGENT EXECUTION)
  |
  |-- Agent shapes the dispatch under its frontmatter constraints
  |-- Streams JSON events (session.started, message.delta, tool.call,
  |    tool.result, session.completed) to the calling AI
  |-- Honors LEAF agent restrictions (no nested dispatches, read-only)
  |
  v
Calling AI (CONDUCTOR — RESULT HANDBACK)
  |
  |-- Parses the JSON event stream
  |-- Validates agent output quality
  |-- Integrates findings into the parent workflow
  |-- Decides next step (next dispatch, completion, escalation)
```

### Invocation pattern

cli-opencode dispatches use the `--agent <slug>` flag. The flag loads the agent definition from `.opencode/agents/<slug>.md` (project-local) or the user-level fallback. The dispatch shape pins model, variant, format, and working directory alongside the agent slug:

```bash
opencode run \
  --model opencode-go/deepseek-v4-pro \
  --agent <slug> \
  --variant high \
  --format json \
  --dir /repo \
  "<prompt>"
```

The `--variant high` flag is the cli-opencode default for cross-AI dispatches. The agent's own frontmatter may override the model selection if the agent pins a specific model. When an agent does NOT pin a model, the `--model` flag wins.

### Conductor rules

1. The calling AI always **decomposes** complex tasks before delegating to a single agent.
2. The calling AI always **validates** OpenCode output before integrating.
3. The calling AI never **blindly forwards** user requests to OpenCode without picking the right agent.
4. OpenCode agents operate within their declared tool-permission boundaries — LEAF agents may not write files or dispatch sub-agents.
5. If an agent returns low-quality output, the calling AI retries with refined instructions or selects a different agent.
6. Each `opencode run` invocation is **stateless** by default — include all necessary context in the prompt or use `--continue` / `--session <id>` for explicit continuation.
7. The calling AI surfaces the JSON event stream to the operator when relevant, especially `tool.call` events.

### Orchestration boundaries

| Concern | Owner |
|---------|-------|
| Task selection and decomposition | Calling AI |
| Agent slug selection | Calling AI |
| Prompt construction (RCAF / TIDD-EC / etc.) | Calling AI |
| Model and variant selection | Calling AI (agent frontmatter may pin) |
| Tool permissions inside the dispatch | Agent frontmatter |
| System prompt and behavioral rules | Agent frontmatter |
| Result validation and integration | Calling AI |
| Memory handback extraction | Calling AI |

---

## 3. AGENT ROSTER

The repo ships these agents under `.opencode/agents/`. The cli-opencode skill can route to any of them. The summary table below is followed by per-agent property tables for the most-dispatched roles.

| Slug | Role | When to dispatch |
|------|------|-------------------|
| `general` | Default subagent — implementation, complex tasks | Routine multi-step work that does not match a specialist |
| `context` | Read-only retrieval and codebase exploration | Search, pattern discovery, context loading. LEAF agent — no sub-dispatches, no writes |
| `orchestrate` | Multi-agent coordination | Complex workflows that span several agents |
| `write` | Documentation generation | Creating READMEs, skills, guides |
| `review` | Code review and PR analysis | Pre-merge reviews, quality gates. READ-ONLY |
| `debug` | Fresh-perspective debugging | Root cause analysis after 3+ failed debug attempts. Exclusive write access for `debug-delegation.md` |
| `deep-research` | Iterative research loop executor | Single research-cycle dispatches. State externalized to JSONL + strategy.md. Dispatched only by `/deep:research` command |
| `deep-review` | Iterative code review loop executor | Single review-cycle dispatches. P0/P1/P2 findings, severity-weighted convergence. Dispatched only by `/deep:review` command |
| `ai-council` | Multi-strategy planning architect | Complex planning that benefits from comparing multiple solution strategies. PLANNING-ONLY |
| `deep-agent-improvement` | Proposal-only mutator for bounded agent improvement | Agent evaluation via `/deep:agent-improvement` command loop |

---

### @context — Read-Only Codebase Explorer

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Role** | Read-only codebase exploration and pattern discovery |
| **Sandbox** | Read-only (no writes, no sub-dispatches) |
| **Modifies files** | Never |
| **LEAF constraint** | YES — single iteration, no nested dispatches |

**Best for:** File discovery, pattern analysis, dependency tracing, architecture mapping, gathering context before implementation.

**Delegate when:** The calling AI needs structured codebase exploration with read-only safety guarantees.

---

### @debug — Fresh-Perspective Debugger

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Role** | Systematic debugging with fresh context |
| **Sandbox** | Workspace-write (bug fixes only) |
| **Modifies files** | Yes (bug fixes plus exclusive write access for `debug-delegation.md`) |
| **LEAF constraint** | No |

**Best for:** Bugs that resist initial debugging (3+ failed attempts), root cause analysis, reproducing elusive errors.

**Delegate when:** The calling AI's own debug attempts have failed. The fresh perspective avoids inheriting wrong assumptions from the parent conversation.

**Methodology:** Observe -> Analyze -> Hypothesize -> Fix.

---

### @review — Code Quality Guardian

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Role** | Code review, severity scoring, security audits |
| **Sandbox** | Read-only |
| **Modifies files** | Never |
| **LEAF constraint** | No |

**Best for:** Pre-merge reviews, security audits, P0 / P1 / P2 severity classification.

**Delegate when:** The calling AI wants a second perspective on code quality, or needs to validate its own implementation from a different model's viewpoint.

---


### @ai-council — Multi-Strategy Planning Architect

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Role** | Multi-strategy planning across diverse thinking lenses |
| **Sandbox** | Read-only |
| **Modifies files** | Never |
| **LEAF constraint** | PLANNING-ONLY (no file modifications) |

**Best for:** Architecture decisions, complex planning that benefits from comparing 3+ solution strategies.

**Delegate when:** The calling AI needs a fundamentally different planning approach or wants to compare strategies before committing to one.

---

### @deep-research and @deep-review — Loop Executors

These two agents are dispatched only by their parent commands. The calling AI does NOT route to them directly.

| Slug | Parent command | Loop role |
|------|----------------|-----------|
| `deep-research` | `/deep:research` | Single research iteration with externalized JSONL + strategy.md state |
| `deep-review` | `/deep:review` | Single review iteration with P0/P1/P2 findings and severity-weighted convergence |

Both are LEAF agents. Each iteration is fresh-context. The parent command owns convergence detection and state continuity.

---

## 4. AGENT ROUTING MATRIX

Pick the agent that matches the task type. Default to `general` when no specialist fits.

| Task type | Agent | Invocation pattern |
|-----------|-------|---------------------|
| Codebase exploration | `context` | `opencode run --agent context --variant high --format json --dir /repo "Map the dependency graph for src/"` |
| Multi-agent coordination | `orchestrate` | `opencode run --agent orchestrate --variant high --format json --dir /repo "Coordinate review and testing of auth module"` |
| Documentation generation | `write` | `opencode run --agent write --variant high --format json --dir /repo "Generate README for the cli-opencode skill"` |
| Code review | `review` | `opencode run --agent review --variant high --format json --dir /repo "Review @src/auth.ts for security issues"` |
| Root cause debugging | `debug` | `opencode run --agent debug --variant high --format json --dir /repo "Debug this error: <error>"` |
| Iterative research loop | `deep-research` | **Command-only.** Dispatch via `/deep:research` (or `/deep:research:auto`). Direct `opencode run --agent deep-research` is forbidden; the parent command owns iteration state, JSONL, and convergence. <!-- F-007-B2-02 --> |
| Iterative code review loop | `deep-review` | **Command-only.** Dispatch via `/deep:review` (or `/deep:review:auto`). Direct `opencode run --agent deep-review` is forbidden; the parent command owns iteration state and severity-weighted convergence. <!-- F-007-B2-02 --> |
| Multi-strategy planning | `ai-council` | `opencode run --agent ai-council --variant high --format json --dir /repo "Plan the authentication redesign — compare three strategies."` |
| Agent improvement | `deep-agent-improvement` | **Command-only.** Dispatch via `/deep:agent-improvement`. Direct `opencode run --agent deep-agent-improvement` is forbidden; the parent command owns evaluation, candidates, and promotion. <!-- F-007-B2-02 --> |
| Default / unspecified | `general` | `opencode run --agent general --variant high --format json --dir /repo "<prompt>"` |

---

## 5. THE `As @<agent>` PROMPT-TIME PATTERN

Mirroring the sibling cli-* skills, cli-opencode supports an inline routing pattern where the calling AI prefixes the prompt with `As @<agent>:` instead of using the `--agent` flag. This is useful when the calling AI dispatches a single `opencode run` and shifts agent behavior mid-session.

```bash
opencode run \
  --model opencode-go/deepseek-v4-pro \
  --variant high \
  --format json \
  --dir /repo \
  "As @review: Inspect @src/auth.ts for security issues. Surface any P0 / P1 findings with file:line citations."
```

The dispatched session parses the `As @review:` prefix and loads the review agent's frontmatter from `.opencode/agents/review.md`. The remaining prompt is passed to that agent.

### When to prefer `--agent` vs `As @<agent>:`

| Pattern | When |
|---------|------|
| `--agent <slug>` | Routine routing — the calling AI knows the right agent up front |
| `As @<agent>:` | The calling AI wants the agent slug to be visible in the prompt for debugging or telemetry |

---

## 6. MULTI-AGENT WORKFLOWS

cli-opencode dispatches that touch multiple agents follow this pattern:

1. Use `--agent orchestrate` for the entry-point dispatch.

1: // MODULE: Deep-Loop Bayesian Scorer
2: 
3: // ───── CORE LOGIC ─────
4: 
5: /**
6:  * Compute a Bayesian estimate of success probability using Laplace smoothing.
7:  *
8:  * @param success - Number of successful outcomes (non-negative integer).
9:  * @param total - Total number of trials (non-negative integer, >= success).
10:  * @returns Smoothed probability: (success + 1) / (total + 2).
11:  * @throws RangeError If inputs are not valid integers or success exceeds total.
12:  */
13: export function computeScore(success: number, total: number): number {
14:   if (!Number.isInteger(success) || !Number.isInteger(total)) {
15:     throw new RangeError('success and total must be integers');
16:   }
17:   if (success < 0 || total < 0) {
18:     throw new RangeError('success and total must be non-negative');
19:   }
20:   if (success > total) {
21:     throw new RangeError('success cannot exceed total');
22:   }
23: 
24:   return (success + 1) / (total + 2);
25: }
26: 
27: /**
28:  * Determine whether a model should be demoted based on score and call count.
29:  *
30:  * @param score - Current Bayesian score (finite number).
31:  * @param totalCalls - Number of calls involving this model (non-negative integer).
32:  * @returns True if score < 0.5 and totalCalls >= 3.
33:  * @throws RangeError If inputs are invalid.
34:  */
35: export function shouldDemote(score: number, totalCalls: number): boolean {
36:   if (!Number.isFinite(score)) {
37:     throw new RangeError('score must be finite');
38:   }
39:   if (!Number.isInteger(totalCalls) || totalCalls < 0) {
40:     throw new RangeError('totalCalls must be a non-negative integer');
41:   }
42: 
43:   return score < 0.5 && totalCalls >= 3;
44: }

83:   clause: string;
84:   params: unknown[];
85: }
86: 
87: // ───── HELPERS ─────
88: 
89: function buildNamespacePredicate(alias: string, ns: Namespace): SqlFragment {
90:   const prefix = alias ? `${alias}.` : '';
91:   const clauses = [`${prefix}spec_folder = ?`, `${prefix}loop_type = ?`];
92:   const params: unknown[] = [ns.specFolder, ns.loopType];
93: 
94:   if (ns.sessionId) {
95:     clauses.push(`${prefix}session_id = ?`);
96:     params.push(ns.sessionId);
97:   }
98: 
99:   return {
100:     clause: clauses.join(' AND '),
101:     params,
102:   };
103: }
104: 
105: function buildCompositeNodeJoin(
106:   nodeAlias: string,
107:   edgeAlias: string,
108:   edgeNodeColumn: 'source_id' | 'target_id',
109: ): string {
110:   return `${nodeAlias}.spec_folder = ${edgeAlias}.spec_folder
111:       AND ${nodeAlias}.loop_type = ${edgeAlias}.loop_type
112:       AND ${nodeAlias}.session_id = ${edgeAlias}.session_id
113:       AND ${nodeAlias}.id = ${edgeAlias}.${edgeNodeColumn}`;
114: }
115: 
116: function parseNodeMetadata(metadata: CoverageNode['metadata'] | string | null | undefined): Record<string, unknown> | null {
117:   if (!metadata) return null;
118:   if (typeof metadata === 'string') {
119:     try {
120:       const parsed = JSON.parse(metadata) as unknown;
121:       return parsed && typeof parsed === 'object' ? parsed as Record<string, unknown> : null;
122:     } catch {
123:       return null;
124:     }
125:   }
126:   return typeof metadata === 'object' ? metadata as Record<string, unknown> : null;
127: }
128: 
129: function buildAnsweringFindingsByQuestion(edges: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalEdgeLike>): Map<string, string[]> {
130:   const answeringFindings = new Map<string, string[]>();
131: 
132:   for (const edge of edges) {
133:     if (edge.relation !== 'ANSWERS') continue;
134:     if (!answeringFindings.has(edge.targetId)) answeringFindings.set(edge.targetId, []);
135:     answeringFindings.get(edge.targetId)!.push(edge.sourceId);
136:   }
137: 
138:   return answeringFindings;
139: }
140: 
141: function buildCitedSourcesByFinding(edges: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalEdgeLike>): Map<string, string[]> {
142:   const citedSources = new Map<string, string[]>();
143: 
144:   for (const edge of edges) {
145:     if (edge.relation !== 'CITES') continue;
146:     if (!citedSources.has(edge.sourceId)) citedSources.set(edge.sourceId, []);
147:     citedSources.get(edge.sourceId)!.push(edge.targetId);
148:   }
149: 
150:   return citedSources;
151: }
152: 
153: /**
154:  * Compute longest-path depth for each node from any root node.
155:  * Mirrors the in-memory CJS implementation so both layers report
156:  * the same structural depth for DAG-shaped coverage graphs.
157:  */
158: function computeDepths(nodes: CoverageNode[], edges: CoverageEdge[]): Map<string, number> {
159:   const adjacency = new Map<string, string[]>();
160:   const inDegree = new Map<string, number>();
161: 
162:   for (const node of nodes) {
163:     adjacency.set(node.id, []);
164:     inDegree.set(node.id, 0);
165:   }
166: 
167:   for (const edge of edges) {
168:     if (!adjacency.has(edge.sourceId)) adjacency.set(edge.sourceId, []);
169:     adjacency.get(edge.sourceId)!.push(edge.targetId);
170:     inDegree.set(edge.targetId, (inDegree.get(edge.targetId) ?? 0) + 1);
171:     if (!inDegree.has(edge.sourceId)) inDegree.set(edge.sourceId, 0);
172:   }
173: 
174:   const depthMap = new Map<string, number>();
175:   const remaining = new Map(inDegree);
176:   const queue: string[] = [];
177: 
178:   for (const [id, degree] of remaining) {
179:     if (degree === 0) {
180:       depthMap.set(id, 0);
181:       queue.push(id);
182:     }
183:   }
184: 
185:   let queueIndex = 0;
186:   while (queueIndex < queue.length) {
187:     const current = queue[queueIndex++];
188:     const currentDepth = depthMap.get(current) ?? 0;
189: 
190:     for (const childId of adjacency.get(current) ?? []) {
191:       const candidateDepth = currentDepth + 1;
192:       if (candidateDepth > (depthMap.get(childId) ?? 0)) {
193:         depthMap.set(childId, candidateDepth);
194:       }
195: 
196:       const nextDegree = (remaining.get(childId) ?? 0) - 1;
197:       remaining.set(childId, nextDegree);
198:       if (nextDegree === 0) {
199:         queue.push(childId);
200:       }
201:     }
202:   }
203: 
204:   for (const node of nodes) {
205:     if (!depthMap.has(node.id)) depthMap.set(node.id, 0);
206:   }
207: 
208:   return depthMap;
209: }
210: 
211: function computeHotspotSaturation(d: Database.Database, ns: Namespace): number {
212:   const nodeNamespace = buildNamespacePredicate('', ns);
213:   const edgeNamespace = buildNamespacePredicate('e', ns);
214:   const files = d.prepare(`
215:     SELECT id, metadata FROM coverage_nodes
216:     WHERE ${nodeNamespace.clause} AND kind = 'FILE'
217:   `).all(...nodeNamespace.params) as Array<{ id: string; metadata: string | null }>;
218: 
219:   const hotspotFiles: string[] = [];
220:   for (const f of files) {
221:     if (f.metadata) {
222:       try {
223:         const meta = JSON.parse(f.metadata);
224:         if (meta.hotspot_score && meta.hotspot_score > 0) {
225:           hotspotFiles.push(f.id);
226:         }
227:       } catch { }
228:     }
229:   }
230: 
231:   if (hotspotFiles.length === 0) return 1.0;
232: 
233:   let saturated = 0;
234:   for (const fileId of hotspotFiles) {
235:     const dimCoverage = (d.prepare(`
236:       SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT e.source_id) as c
237:       FROM coverage_edges e
238:       JOIN coverage_nodes n ON ${buildCompositeNodeJoin('n', 'e', 'source_id')}
239:       WHERE ${edgeNamespace.clause}
240:         AND e.target_id = ?
241:         AND e.relation = 'COVERS'
242:         AND n.kind = 'DIMENSION'
243:     `).get(...edgeNamespace.params, fileId) as { c: number }).c;
244: 
245:     if (dimCoverage >= 2) saturated++;
246:   }
247: 
248:   return hotspotFiles.length > 0 ? saturated / hotspotFiles.length : 1.0;
249: }
250: 
251: // ───── CORE LOGIC ─────
252: 
253: /**
254:  * Compute degree, depth, and weight signals for all nodes in a namespace.
255:  *
256:  * @param ns - Namespace identifying the coverage graph.
257:  * @returns Array of node-level signal objects.
258:  */
259: export function computeNodeSignals(ns: Namespace): NodeSignal[] {
260:   const nodes = getNodes(ns);
261:   const edges = getEdges(ns);
262: 
263:   const inDegreeMap = new Map<string, number>();
264:   const outDegreeMap = new Map<string, number>();
265:   const weightSumMap = new Map<string, number>();
266: 
267:   for (const edge of edges) {
268:     outDegreeMap.set(edge.sourceId, (outDegreeMap.get(edge.sourceId) ?? 0) + 1);
269:     inDegreeMap.set(edge.targetId, (inDegreeMap.get(edge.targetId) ?? 0) + 1);
270:     weightSumMap.set(edge.sourceId, (weightSumMap.get(edge.sourceId) ?? 0) + edge.weight);
271:     weightSumMap.set(edge.targetId, (weightSumMap.get(edge.targetId) ?? 0) + edge.weight);
272:   }
273: 
274:   const depthMap = computeDepths(nodes, edges);
275: 
276:   return nodes.map(node => ({
277:     nodeId: node.id,
278:     kind: node.kind,
279:     degree: (inDegreeMap.get(node.id) ?? 0) + (outDegreeMap.get(node.id) ?? 0),
280:     inDegree: inDegreeMap.get(node.id) ?? 0,
281:     outDegree: outDegreeMap.get(node.id) ?? 0,
282:     depth: depthMap.get(node.id) ?? 0,
283:     weightSum: weightSumMap.get(node.id) ?? 0,
284:   }));
285: }
286: 
287: /**
288:  * Canonical research question coverage: questions with at least two ANSWERS
289:  * edges divided by all research questions in the graph.
290:  *
291:  * @param nodes - Research nodes in the graph.
292:  * @param edges - Research edges in the graph.
293:  * @returns Coverage ratio in [0, 1].
294:  */
295: export function computeResearchQuestionCoverageFromData(
296:   nodes: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalNodeLike>,
297:   edges: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalEdgeLike>,
298: ): number {
299:   const questionIds = nodes
300:     .filter(node => node.kind === 'QUESTION')
301:     .map(node => node.id);
302: 
303:   if (questionIds.length === 0) return 0;
304: 
305:   const answeringFindings = buildAnsweringFindingsByQuestion(edges);
306:   let coveredQuestions = 0;
307: 
308:   for (const questionId of questionIds) {
309:     if ((answeringFindings.get(questionId) ?? []).length >= 2) {
310:       coveredQuestions++;
311:     }
312:   }
313: 
314:   return coveredQuestions / questionIds.length;
315: }
316: 
317: /**
318:  * Canonical research claim verification rate: verified claims divided by all
319:  * claim nodes, where verified means verification_status exists and is not
320:  * "unresolved".

</content>

179: 
180: export const VALID_KINDS: Record<LoopType, readonly string[]> = {
181:   research: ['QUESTION', 'FINDING', 'CLAIM', 'SOURCE'] as const,
182:   review: [

<content>
1: ---
2: name: deep-loop-runtime
3: version: 1.4.0
4: description: "Shared deep-loop runtime: executor + prompt-pack + validation + atomic state + coverage-graph + Bayesian scoring + fallback routing."
5: allowed-tools: [Bash, Read, Glob, Grep]
6: ---
7: 
8: # Deep Loop Runtime
9: 
10: Shared runtime infrastructure for the deep-review, deep-research, and deep-context loop workflows (and deep-ai-council via the council-scoped modules). The FULL_ISOLATE_NO_MCP consolidation moved the shared runtime libraries, coverage-graph ownership, script entry points, SQLite storage, and runtime tests into this peer skill.
11: 
12: ---
13: 
14: ## 1. WHEN TO USE
15: 
16: This skill is consumed by the deep-review, deep-research, and deep-context workflow YAML and script paths (and the deep-ai-council council modules), not invoked directly as a user-facing workflow.
17: 
18: Use it when a deep-loop workflow needs shared runtime support for:
19: 
20: - executor configuration and audit metadata
21: - prompt-pack rendering
22: - post-dispatch validation
23: - atomic state files and JSONL repair
24: - loop locking and permissions gates
25: - Bayesian scoring and fallback routing
26: - coverage-graph schema, query, and signal interpretation
27: 
28: ### Activation Triggers
29: 
30: This skill is activated when:
31: 
32: - A deep-review or deep-research workflow YAML invokes `node .opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/scripts/<name>.cjs`
33: - A test file imports `from '.../deep-loop-runtime/lib/...'`
34: - An operator queries / mutates the coverage graph via the runtime scripts
35: - A workflow needs Bayesian convergence scoring or fallback executor routing
36: 
37: ### When NOT to Use
38: 
39: Do not invoke this skill when:
40: 
41: - A user requests an interactive command (use `/deep:review` or `/deep:research` instead — those orchestrate this runtime)
42: - A package outside deep-review / deep-research needs deep-loop semantics (out of supported scope; open a packet to extend ownership)
43: - An MCP tool surface is needed (this skill explicitly has none — the isolation ADR removed the deep-loop MCP tools)
44: 
45: ---
46: 
47: ## 2. SMART ROUTING
48: 
49: This skill has no direct LLM-facing keyword triggers — it is invoked by workflow YAMLs (via `bash:` script calls) and by test imports. The router below documents which sub-domain to load for a given consumer query.
50: 
51: ### Primary Detection Signal
52: 
53: ```bash
54: request_text="$(printf '%s' "$USER_REQUEST" | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]')"
55: case "$request_text" in
56:   *"deep-loop runtime"*|*"deep-loop-runtime"*|*"executor config"*|*"prompt pack"*) DEEP_LOOP_INTENT=1 ;;
57:   *"coverage graph"*|*"deep_loop_graph"*|*"bayesian scorer"*|*"loop lock"*) DEEP_LOOP_INTENT=1 ;;
58:   *"fallback router"*|*"post-dispatch validate"*|*"jsonl repair"*) DEEP_LOOP_INTENT=1 ;;
59:   *) DEEP_LOOP_INTENT=0 ;;
60: esac
61: ```
62: 
63: ### Phase Detection
64: 
65: ```text
66: TASK CONTEXT
67:     |
68:     +- STEP 0: Detect runtime-call intent vs. consumer-orchestration intent
69:     +- STEP 1: If consumer (workflow YAML), invoke the relevant scripts/*.cjs
70:     +- STEP 2: If contributor (modifying lib code), load matching lib/ subtree
71:     +- STEP 3: If test author, load tests/ + lib/ pair
72: ```
73: 
74: ### Resource Domains
75: 
76: ```text
77: lib/deep-loop/*.ts            # 10 runtime modules (executor, prompt-pack, validation, state, scoring, routing)
78: lib/coverage-graph/*.ts       # 3 schema + query + signals modules
79: scripts/*.cjs                 # 4 entry points (convergence, upsert, query, status)
80: database/*.sqlite              # runtime-owned SQLite (deep-loop-graph.sqlite)
81: tests/{unit,integration,lifecycle}/  # 21+ vitest files split by responsibility
82: ```
83: 
84: ### Resource Loading Levels
85: 
86: | Tier | When loaded | Purpose |
87: |------|-------------|---------|
88: | ALWAYS | SKILL.md frontmatter | description, allowed-tools, version |
89: | CONDITIONAL | When a runtime call dispatches | `lib/` modules required by the called function |
90: | ON_DEMAND | When a test fails or a contributor extends | `tests/` + matching `lib/` sub-tree |
91: 
92: ### Smart Router Pseudocode
93: 
94: ```text
95: IF request mentions a specific lib name (e.g. "bayesian-scorer", "prompt-pack"):
96:   → Load lib/deep-loop/<name>.ts + matching tests/unit/<name>.vitest.ts
97: ELIF request mentions coverage-graph / convergence / signals:
98:   → Load lib/coverage-graph/*.ts + tests/integration/review-depth-*.vitest.ts
99: ELIF request mentions a script (convergence.cjs, status.cjs, etc.):
100:   → Load scripts/<name>.cjs + tests/integration/<name>-script.vitest.ts + lib/coverage-graph/coverage-graph-db.ts
101: ELSE:
102:   → Defer to consumer skill (deep-review / deep-research)
103: ```
104: 
105: ---
106: 
107: ## 3. HOW IT WORKS
108: 
109: ### Runtime Architecture
110: 
111: The runtime exposes deep-loop primitives to consumer workflows through two paths: **direct `.cjs` script invocation** (replacing the removed `mcp__mk_spec_memory__deep_loop_graph_*` MCP tools) and **TypeScript library imports** (from workflow scripts and tests).
112: 
113: ```text
114: [deep-review/deep-research workflow YAML]
115:             │
116:             ├─ bash: node .../scripts/<name>.cjs   (coverage-graph mutations)
117:             │       └─→ lib/coverage-graph/*.ts → database/deep-loop-graph.sqlite
118:             │
119:             └─ ts-import: lib/deep-loop/<lib>.ts   (runtime primitives)
120:                     └─→ atomic-state, prompt-pack, post-dispatch-validate, etc.
121: ```
122: 
123: ### Library Structure
124: 
125: **`lib/deep-loop/` (10 modules):**
126: - `executor-config.ts` — schema + parsing for per-iteration executor settings
127: - `executor-audit.ts` — appends `executor` block to iteration JSONL (provenance)
128: - `prompt-pack.ts` — renders the iteration prompt template
129: - `post-dispatch-validate.ts` — validates iteration outputs (markdown + JSONL + delta)
130: - `atomic-state.ts` — atomic state-log writes
131: - `jsonl-repair.ts` — recovers corrupt JSONL state lines
132: - `loop-lock.ts` — single-writer locking
133: - `permissions-gate.ts` — permission scope checks
134: - `bayesian-scorer.ts` — convergence scoring
135: - `fallback-router.ts` — executor fallback decision matrix
136: 
137: **`lib/coverage-graph/` (3 modules):**
138: - `coverage-graph-db.ts` — SQLite schema + node-kind allow-list + connection lifecycle
139: - `coverage-graph-query.ts` — query builders
140: - `coverage-graph-signals.ts` — convergence signal extraction
141: 
142: ### Council Primitives
143: 
144: The Runtime Boundary Decision (ADR-001) extends `deep-loop-runtime/lib/` with council-compatible infrastructure primitives while keeping operator-facing and domain workflow semantics in `deep-ai-council`. These primitives are consumed by downstream deep-ai-council orchestration (per-topic multi-round, session-findings registry, command and skill wiring, parity and cost docs) for per-topic orchestration, multi-topic session state, command wiring, parity tests and docs.
145: 
146: **`lib/council/` (5 modules):**
147: - `multi-seat-dispatch.cjs` — runs seat executors in parallel for one council round, preserves seat result order, and returns fulfilled/rejected per-seat outcomes plus round summary counts.
148: - `round-state-jsonl.cjs` — appends per-round JSONL records with a lock-file single-writer guard, repairs corrupt trailing JSONL before append, fsyncs writes, and exposes round-state readers for resume.
149: - `adjudicator-verdict-scoring.cjs` — scores Round-N -> Round-N+1 adjudicator verdict deltas using ADR-003 weights for option change, confidence delta, material-risk Jaccard delta, axis flip rate, and blocking-disagreement delta.
150: - `cost-guards.cjs` — normalizes and enforces ADR-004 defaults for `max_rounds_per_topic`, `max_topics_per_session`, `saturation_threshold`, and `seats_per_round`, including upper-bound seat-output calculation.
151: - `session-state-hierarchy.cjs` — creates and validates the ADR-002 session -> topic -> round state shape, including stable `topic-NNN-slug` and `round-NNN` ids.
152: 
153: The existing `lib/deep-loop/atomic-state.ts`, `lib/deep-loop/jsonl-repair.ts`, and `lib/deep-loop/loop-lock.ts` remain the sibling runtime contracts. Council modules mirror those durability semantics in a council-scoped CJS surface so downstream `deep-ai-council` phases can consume them without modifying deep-review or deep-research behavior.
154: 
155: ### Script Entry Points
156: 
157: Four `.cjs` scripts replace the 4 deleted MCP tools, each honoring the same JSON-in / JSON-out contract documented in ADR-001 (the script interface contract):
158: 
159: | Script | Replaces | Purpose | Exit codes |
160: |--------|----------|---------|------------|
161: | `scripts/convergence.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_convergence` | Computes typed CONTINUE / STOP_ALLOWED / STOP_BLOCKED decisions | 0=ok, 1=script, 2=DB, 3=input |
162: | `scripts/upsert.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_upsert` | Stores nodes + edges from iteration `graphEvents` | 0=ok, 1=script, 2=DB, 3=input |
163: | `scripts/query.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_query` | Inspects uncovered questions, unverified claims, contradictions | 0=ok, 1=script, 2=DB, 3=input |
164: | `scripts/status.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_status` | Session-scoped health report | 0=ok, 1=script, 2=DB, 3=input |
165: | `scripts/fanout-pool.cjs` | n/a (new) | Concurrency-capped worker pool + status ledger for fan-out lineages | exports only (no main) |
166: | `scripts/fanout-run.cjs` | n/a (new) | CLI lineage pool driver — spawns N headless CLI subprocesses (codex, claude, opencode), each running the full loop in `lineages/{label}/`; salvage sweep via fanout-salvage.cjs after each subprocess exits | 0=all ok, 2=some failed, 3=all failed |
167: | `scripts/fanout-salvage.cjs` | n/a (new) | Write-failure salvage: recovers missing iteration .md files from captured subprocess stdout; per-sessionId isolation preserved | exports only (no main) |
168: | `scripts/fanout-merge.cjs` | n/a (new) | Cross-lineage merge: research (dedup by id + attribution) or review (strongest-restriction P0 rollup) → consolidated registry + fanout-attribution.md | 0=ok, 3=input |
169: 
170: **Fan-out isolation invariant:** each CLI lineage receives its own `{base}/lineages/{label}/` artifact dir (via `config.fanout_lineage_artifact_dir` override in `step_resolve_artifact_root`) and its own `session_id` for `convergence.cjs` + coverage-graph DB writes (PK includes session_id — no schema change, no collision). Same-kind replicas get distinct `SPECKIT_<KIND>_STATE_DIR` values to prevent lockfile contention.
171: 
172: Each script: parses argv → opens `database/deep-loop-graph.sqlite` → calls the lib function → writes JSON to stdout → closes DB in `finally` → exits with the appropriate code.
173: 
174: ### Test Organization
175: 
176: ```text
177: tests/
178: ├── unit/         # 13 files — per-module unit tests (atomic-state, executor-config, etc.)
179: ├── integration/  # 7 files — script invocations + review-depth fixtures
180: ├── lifecycle/    # 1 file — db-open-close pattern verification
181: └── _helpers/     # 1 file — spawn-cjs helper for direct-invocation tests
182: ```
183: 
184: The system-spec-kit `mcp_server/vitest.config.ts` includes `'../deep-loop-runtime/tests/**/*.{vitest,test}.ts'` so a single vitest run discovers both surfaces.
185: 
186: ---
187: 
188: ## 4. RULES
189: 
190: ### ALWAYS
191: 
192: - **ALWAYS** open the SQLite DB inside a `try` block and close it in a `finally` block (script lifecycle invariant per ADR-001, the script interface contract)
193: - **ALWAYS** emit JSON-only output to stdout from `scripts/*.cjs` (workflow YAML parses stdout for output bindings)
194: - **ALWAYS** include `'use strict';` on line 2 of every `.cjs` script (per sk-code OPENCODE JS style)
195: - **ALWAYS** include a `MODULE: <name>` header comment in every `lib/**/*.ts` file (per sk-code OPENCODE TS-MODULE-HEADER convention)
196: - **ALWAYS** preserve atomic-state semantics when mutating JSONL state — never partial-write
197: - **ALWAYS** acquire `loop-lock` before any state-log mutation
198: 
199: ### NEVER
200: 
201: - **NEVER** register MCP tools from this skill — the isolation ADR explicitly removed the MCP surface; reintroducing tools defeats the FULL_ISOLATE direction
202: - **NEVER** open the SQLite DB outside `lib/coverage-graph/coverage-graph-db.ts` — single owner of the connection
203: - **NEVER** import from `system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/deep-loop/` or `system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/coverage-graph/` — those locations are empty after the runtime moved here; import from this skill instead
204: - **NEVER** write to `database/deep-loop-graph.sqlite` without holding the `loop-lock`
205: - **NEVER** swallow exceptions in script entry points — propagate to the exit-code matrix
206: 
207: ### ESCALATE IF
208: 
209: - **ESCALATE** to the user if a runtime call requires a new MCP tool — the architectural direction prohibits MCP additions; the user must decide on policy override
210: - **ESCALATE** to a new ownership ADR if a new consumer skill (beyond deep-review and deep-research) needs deep-loop runtime — extension requires a new ownership ADR
211: - **ESCALATE** if the SQLite schema requires a backward-incompatible change — coordinate with all consumer workflows + add migration to `coverage-graph-db.ts`
212: - **ESCALATE** if a script's stdout JSON contract changes — every workflow YAML output binding depends on the existing shape
213: 
214: ---
215: 
216: ## 5. SCOPE
217: 
218: In scope:
219: 
220: - runtime libraries under `lib/deep-loop/`
221: - coverage-graph runtime libraries under `lib/coverage-graph/`
222: - `.cjs` script entry points under `scripts/`
223: - runtime-owned SQLite storage under `database/`
224: - runtime tests under `tests/`
225: 
226: Out of scope:
227: 
228: - MCP tool registration
229: - MCP handler ownership
230: - DB lifecycle inside `system-spec-kit/mcp_server/`
231: - direct user command routing
232: 
233: ---
234: 
235: ## 6. ARCHITECTURE
236: 
237: - `lib/deep-loop/` holds shared deep-loop runtime libraries.
238: - `lib/coverage-graph/` holds coverage-graph schema, query, and signal helpers.
239: - `scripts/` holds `.cjs` entry points used by workflow YAML and collateral commands.
240: - `database/` owns relocated runtime SQLite artifacts.

(Showing lines 1-240 of 296. Use offset=241 to continue.)
</content>

# Deep Loop Runtime

Shared runtime infrastructure for the deep-review, deep-research, and deep-context loop workflows (and deep-ai-council via the council-scoped modules). The FULL_ISOLATE_NO_MCP consolidation moved the shared runtime libraries, coverage-graph ownership, script entry points, SQLite storage, and runtime tests into this peer skill.

---

## 1. WHEN TO USE

This skill is consumed by the deep-review, deep-research, and deep-context workflow YAML and script paths (and the deep-ai-council council modules), not invoked directly as a user-facing workflow.

Use it when a deep-loop workflow needs shared runtime support for:

- executor configuration and audit metadata
- prompt-pack rendering
- post-dispatch validation
- atomic state files and JSONL repair
- loop locking and permissions gates
- Bayesian scoring and fallback routing
- coverage-graph schema, query, and signal interpretation

### Activation Triggers

This skill is activated when:

- A deep-review or deep-research workflow YAML invokes `node .opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/scripts/<name>.cjs`
- A test file imports `from '.../deep-loop-runtime/lib/...'`
- An operator queries / mutates the coverage graph via the runtime scripts
- A workflow needs Bayesian convergence scoring or fallback executor routing

### When NOT to Use

Do not invoke this skill when:

- A user requests an interactive command (use `/deep:review` or `/deep:research` instead — those orchestrate this runtime)
- A package outside deep-review / deep-research needs deep-loop semantics (out of supported scope; open a packet to extend ownership)
- An MCP tool surface is needed (this skill explicitly has none — the isolation ADR removed the deep-loop MCP tools)

---

## 2. SMART ROUTING

This skill has no direct LLM-facing keyword triggers — it is invoked by workflow YAMLs (via `bash:` script calls) and by test imports. The router below documents which sub-domain to load for a given consumer query.

### Primary Detection Signal

```bash
request_text="$(printf '%s' "$USER_REQUEST" | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]')"
case "$request_text" in
  *"deep-loop runtime"*|*"deep-loop-runtime"*|*"executor config"*|*"prompt pack"*) DEEP_LOOP_INTENT=1 ;;
  *"coverage graph"*|*"deep_loop_graph"*|*"bayesian scorer"*|*"loop lock"*) DEEP_LOOP_INTENT=1 ;;
  *"fallback router"*|*"post-dispatch validate"*|*"jsonl repair"*) DEEP_LOOP_INTENT=1 ;;
  *) DEEP_LOOP_INTENT=0 ;;
esac
```

### Phase Detection

```text
TASK CONTEXT
    |
    +- STEP 0: Detect runtime-call intent vs. consumer-orchestration intent
    +- STEP 1: If consumer (workflow YAML), invoke the relevant scripts/*.cjs
    +- STEP 2: If contributor (modifying lib code), load matching lib/ subtree
    +- STEP 3: If test author, load tests/ + lib/ pair
```

### Resource Domains

```text
lib/deep-loop/*.ts            # 10 runtime modules (executor, prompt-pack, validation, state, scoring, routing)
lib/coverage-graph/*.ts       # 3 schema + query + signals modules
scripts/*.cjs                 # 4 entry points (convergence, upsert, query, status)
database/*.sqlite              # runtime-owned SQLite (deep-loop-graph.sqlite)
tests/{unit,integration,lifecycle}/  # 21+ vitest files split by responsibility
```

### Resource Loading Levels

| Tier | When loaded | Purpose |
|------|-------------|---------|
| ALWAYS | SKILL.md frontmatter | description, allowed-tools, version |
| CONDITIONAL | When a runtime call dispatches | `lib/` modules required by the called function |
| ON_DEMAND | When a test fails or a contributor extends | `tests/` + matching `lib/` sub-tree |

### Smart Router Pseudocode

```text
IF request mentions a specific lib name (e.g. "bayesian-scorer", "prompt-pack"):
  → Load lib/deep-loop/<name>.ts + matching tests/unit/<name>.vitest.ts
ELIF request mentions coverage-graph / convergence / signals:
  → Load lib/coverage-graph/*.ts + tests/integration/review-depth-*.vitest.ts
ELIF request mentions a script (convergence.cjs, status.cjs, etc.):
  → Load scripts/<name>.cjs + tests/integration/<name>-script.vitest.ts + lib/coverage-graph/coverage-graph-db.ts
ELSE:
  → Defer to consumer skill (deep-review / deep-research)
```

---

## 3. HOW IT WORKS

### Runtime Architecture

The runtime exposes deep-loop primitives to consumer workflows through two paths: **direct `.cjs` script invocation** (replacing the removed `mcp__mk_spec_memory__deep_loop_graph_*` MCP tools) and **TypeScript library imports** (from workflow scripts and tests).

```text
[deep-review/deep-research workflow YAML]
            │
            ├─ bash: node .../scripts/<name>.cjs   (coverage-graph mutations)
            │       └─→ lib/coverage-graph/*.ts → database/deep-loop-graph.sqlite
            │
            └─ ts-import: lib/deep-loop/<lib>.ts   (runtime primitives)
                    └─→ atomic-state, prompt-pack, post-dispatch-validate, etc.
```

### Library Structure

**`lib/deep-loop/` (10 modules):**
- `executor-config.ts` — schema + parsing for per-iteration executor settings
- `executor-audit.ts` — appends `executor` block to iteration JSONL (provenance)
- `prompt-pack.ts` — renders the iteration prompt template
- `post-dispatch-validate.ts` — validates iteration outputs (markdown + JSONL + delta)
- `atomic-state.ts` — atomic state-log writes
- `jsonl-repair.ts` — recovers corrupt JSONL state lines
- `loop-lock.ts` — single-writer locking
- `permissions-gate.ts` — permission scope checks
- `bayesian-scorer.ts` — convergence scoring
- `fallback-router.ts` — executor fallback decision matrix

**`lib/coverage-graph/` (3 modules):**
- `coverage-graph-db.ts` — SQLite schema + node-kind allow-list + connection lifecycle
- `coverage-graph-query.ts` — query builders
- `coverage-graph-signals.ts` — convergence signal extraction

### Council Primitives

The Runtime Boundary Decision (ADR-001) extends `deep-loop-runtime/lib/` with council-compatible infrastructure primitives while keeping operator-facing and domain workflow semantics in `deep-ai-council`. These primitives are consumed by downstream deep-ai-council orchestration (per-topic multi-round, session-findings registry, command and skill wiring, parity and cost docs) for per-topic orchestration, multi-topic session state, command wiring, parity tests and docs.

**`lib/council/` (5 modules):**
- `multi-seat-dispatch.cjs` — runs seat executors in parallel for one council round, preserves seat result order, and returns fulfilled/rejected per-seat outcomes plus round summary counts.
- `round-state-jsonl.cjs` — appends per-round JSONL records with a lock-file single-writer guard, repairs corrupt trailing JSONL before append, fsyncs writes, and exposes round-state readers for resume.
- `adjudicator-verdict-scoring.cjs` — scores Round-N -> Round-N+1 adjudicator verdict deltas using ADR-003 weights for option change, confidence delta, material-risk Jaccard delta, axis flip rate, and blocking-disagreement delta.
- `cost-guards.cjs` — normalizes and enforces ADR-004 defaults for `max_rounds_per_topic`, `max_topics_per_session`, `saturation_threshold`, and `seats_per_round`, including upper-bound seat-output calculation.
- `session-state-hierarchy.cjs` — creates and validates the ADR-002 session -> topic -> round state shape, including stable `topic-NNN-slug` and `round-NNN` ids.

The existing `lib/deep-loop/atomic-state.ts`, `lib/deep-loop/jsonl-repair.ts`, and `lib/deep-loop/loop-lock.ts` remain the sibling runtime contracts. Council modules mirror those durability semantics in a council-scoped CJS surface so downstream `deep-ai-council` phases can consume them without modifying deep-review or deep-research behavior.

### Script Entry Points

Four `.cjs` scripts replace the 4 deleted MCP tools, each honoring the same JSON-in / JSON-out contract documented in ADR-001 (the script interface contract):

| Script | Replaces | Purpose | Exit codes |
|--------|----------|---------|------------|
| `scripts/convergence.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_convergence` | Computes typed CONTINUE / STOP_ALLOWED / STOP_BLOCKED decisions | 0=ok, 1=script, 2=DB, 3=input |
| `scripts/upsert.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_upsert` | Stores nodes + edges from iteration `graphEvents` | 0=ok, 1=script, 2=DB, 3=input |
| `scripts/query.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_query` | Inspects uncovered questions, unverified claims, contradictions | 0=ok, 1=script, 2=DB, 3=input |
| `scripts/status.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_status` | Session-scoped health report | 0=ok, 1=script, 2=DB, 3=input |
| `scripts/fanout-pool.cjs` | n/a (new) | Concurrency-capped worker pool + status ledger for fan-out lineages | exports only (no main) |
| `scripts/fanout-run.cjs` | n/a (new) | CLI lineage pool driver — spawns N headless CLI subprocesses (codex, claude, opencode), each running the full loop in `lineages/{label}/`; salvage sweep via fanout-salvage.cjs after each subprocess exits | 0=all ok, 2=some failed, 3=all failed |
| `scripts/fanout-salvage.cjs` | n/a (new) | Write-failure salvage: recovers missing iteration .md files from captured subprocess stdout; per-sessionId isolation preserved | exports only (no main) |
| `scripts/fanout-merge.cjs` | n/a (new) | Cross-lineage merge: research (dedup by id + attribution) or review (strongest-restriction P0 rollup) → consolidated registry + fanout-attribution.md | 0=ok, 3=input |

**Fan-out isolation invariant:** each CLI lineage receives its own `{base}/lineages/{label}/` artifact dir (via `config.fanout_lineage_artifact_dir` override in `step_resolve_artifact_root`) and its own `session_id` for `convergence.cjs` + coverage-graph DB writes (PK includes session_id — no schema change, no collision). Same-kind replicas get distinct `SPECKIT_<KIND>_STATE_DIR` values to prevent lockfile contention.

Each script: parses argv → opens `database/deep-loop-graph.sqlite` → calls the lib function → writes JSON to stdout → closes DB in `finally` → exits with the appropriate code.

### Test Organization

```text
tests/
├── unit/         # 13 files — per-module unit tests (atomic-state, executor-config, etc.)
├── integration/  # 7 files — script invocations + review-depth fixtures
├── lifecycle/    # 1 file — db-open-close pattern verification
└── _helpers/     # 1 file — spawn-cjs helper for direct-invocation tests
```

The system-spec-kit `mcp_server/vitest.config.ts` includes `'../deep-loop-runtime/tests/**/*.{vitest,test}.ts'` so a single vitest run discovers both surfaces.

---

## 4. RULES

### ALWAYS

- **ALWAYS** open the SQLite DB inside a `try` block and close it in a `finally` block (script lifecycle invariant per ADR-001, the script interface contract)
- **ALWAYS** emit JSON-only output to stdout from `scripts/*.cjs` (workflow YAML parses stdout for output bindings)
- **ALWAYS** include `'use strict';` on line 2 of every `.cjs` script (per sk-code OPENCODE JS style)
- **ALWAYS** include a `MODULE: <name>` header comment in every `lib/**/*.ts` file (per sk-code OPENCODE TS-MODULE-HEADER convention)
- **ALWAYS** preserve atomic-state semantics when mutating JSONL state — never partial-write
- **ALWAYS** acquire `loop-lock` before any state-log mutation

### NEVER

- **NEVER** register MCP tools from this skill — the isolation ADR explicitly removed the MCP surface; reintroducing tools defeats the FULL_ISOLATE direction
- **NEVER** open the SQLite DB outside `lib/coverage-graph/coverage-graph-db.ts` — single owner of the connection
- **NEVER** import from `system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/deep-loop/` or `system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/coverage-graph/` — those locations are empty after the runtime moved here; import from this skill instead
- **NEVER** write to `database/deep-loop-graph.sqlite` without holding the `loop-lock`
- **NEVER** swallow exceptions in script entry points — propagate to the exit-code matrix

### ESCALATE IF

- **ESCALATE** to the user if a runtime call requires a new MCP tool — the architectural direction prohibits MCP additions; the user must decide on policy override
- **ESCALATE** to a new ownership ADR if a new consumer skill (beyond deep-review and deep-research) needs deep-loop runtime — extension requires a new ownership ADR
- **ESCALATE** if the SQLite schema requires a backward-incompatible change — coordinate with all consumer workflows + add migration to `coverage-graph-db.ts`
- **ESCALATE** if a script's stdout JSON contract changes — every workflow YAML output binding depends on the existing shape

---

## 5. SCOPE

In scope:

- runtime libraries under `lib/deep-loop/`
- coverage-graph runtime libraries under `lib/coverage-graph/`
- `.cjs` script entry points under `scripts/`
- runtime-owned SQLite storage under `database/`
- runtime tests under `tests/`

Out of scope:

- MCP tool registration
- MCP handler ownership
- DB lifecycle inside `system-spec-kit/mcp_server/`
- direct user command routing

---

## 6. ARCHITECTURE

- `lib/deep-loop/` holds shared deep-loop runtime libraries.
- `lib/coverage-graph/` holds coverage-graph schema, query, and signal helpers.
- `scripts/` holds `.cjs` entry points used by workflow YAML and collateral commands.
- `database/` owns relocated runtime SQLite artifacts.
